sourceMovie.title: Akira
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Akira (1988) stuck because it commits to its animation premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Katsuhiro Otomo's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The animation and science fiction blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Ghost in the Shell 2.0
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Akira clicked for you, Ghost in the Shell 2.0 (2008) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and science fiction, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Appleseed Alpha
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Akira clicked for you, Appleseed Alpha (2014) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and science fiction, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Akira?
faq.answer: Try Ghost in the Shell 2.0, Appleseed Alpha, BLAME!—each shares DNA with Akira while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Akira?
faq.answer: Akira (1988) is best described as Animation, Science Fiction, Action. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Akira worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.9/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this animation lane.
